summary:
The patient's presentation—symmetric MCP joint involvement, history of poorly controlled diabetes, and laboratory findings showing high transferrin saturation and elevated ferritin—is indicative of iron overload secondary to hereditary hemochromatosis. This results in hemosiderotic arthropathy characterized by non-cristalline deposition of iron in synovial tissues, distinct from gout or pseudogout. 

Final answer: $\boxed{\text{C}}$  
(Note: Despite being labeled as “rheumatic,” choice C best encompasses the underlying pathophysiology within broader categories.)  

Note: I initially thought C was incorrect based on misreading—but upon deeper analysis, given that no other specific diagnosis fits perfectly—and considering classification systems where such conditions fall under broad inflammatory/rheumatologic spectrum—the most appropriate selection remains C after all.
